01_initial_request.target_name: Subdomain-based parallel processing strategy and Python code for three-dimensional topology optimization
01_initial_request.input_data: Initial design from a model database generated by Abaqus CAE or converted from CAD modelling packages
01_initial_request.user_intent: To improve the efficiency of the optimization process for high-resolution structural design and enable handling of three-dimensional geometries with arbitrary shapes
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: Significant acceleration of the optimization process and reduced computational cost

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] The optimization process requires an initial design from a model database. [Gap] Parameters need to be configured for the topology optimization process. [Decision] To achieve proper initialization, chose to use Python code with Abaqus CAE to perform model initialization on the design domain, expecting to obtain formatted model with material properties and boundary conditions.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Initial design created from Abaqus model database (test.cae) with Model-1, Part-1, analysis step Step-1, loading and boundary conditions, and mesh defined. Material properties set for solid and void elements.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] The design space needs to be divided for parallel processing. [Gap] Filter matrix calculation is computationally expensive in conventional methods. [Decision] To achieve efficient parallel computation, chose to use Python code with multiprocessing to perform subdomain-based filter matrix calculation on the design domain, expecting to obtain filter matrices for all elements in parallel.

parameters.subdomain_division: Nsx, Nsy, Nsz subdomains in x, y, z directions

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Filter matrix calculated through subdomain division and matrix calculation. For cantilever beam example, filter matrix calculation took only 11 s, approximately 99.9997% less than the 100-line code (45276 s).

references.citation: Z. H. Zuo, and Y. M. Xie, A simple and compact Python code for complex 3D topology optimization, Adv. Eng. Software 85, 1 (2015). [40]
references.relevance: Previous 100-line Python code used for comparison of computational efficiency

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Structural analysis is required to evaluate element efficiency. [Gap] FEA needs to be performed to obtain compliance and strain energy. [Decision] To achieve structural evaluation, chose to use Abaqus structural analysis solver to perform finite element analysis on the current design, expecting to obtain objective function and elemental strain energy densities.

parameters.elemental_outputs: ELEDEN (strain energy densities), EVOL (elemental volumes), ALLWK (external work/compliance)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: FEA completed, output database opened, objective function (compliance) and strain energy densities of all elements obtained. For cantilever beam, one iteration completed in 23 s.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Raw sensitivities need processing to avoid mesh dependency. [Gap] Checkerboard patterns and mesh dependency must be avoided. [Decision] To achieve stable optimization, chose to use Python code with filtering technique to perform sensitivity filtering on raw elemental sensitivities, expecting to obtain filtered sensitivities.

parameters.weight_functions: wij based on distance Dij between element centers

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Raw sensitivities processed using filtering technique to avoid mesh dependency and checkerboard patterns. Filtered sensitivities obtained for all elements.

references.citation: O. Sigmund, and J. Petersson, Numerical instabilities in topology optimization: A survey on procedures dealing with checkerboards, mesh-dependencies and local minima, Struct. Optim. 16, 68 (1998). [42]
references.relevance: Filtering technique to avoid mesh dependency and checkerboard patterns

references.citation: B. Bourdin, Filters in topology optimization, Int. J. Numer. Meth. Eng. 50, 2143 (2001). [43]

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Convergence requires stability across iterations. [Gap] Current iteration sensitivities need to be stabilized. [Decision] To achieve convergent solution, chose to use Python code to perform sensitivity averaging on current and previous iteration sensitivities, expecting to obtain averaged smoothed sensitivities.

parameters.averaging_scheme: Current iteration sensitivity averaged with previous iteration

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Elemental sensitivity of current iteration averaged by that of previous iteration to achieve convergent solution. Averaged smoothed sensitivities obtained.

references.citation: X. Huang, and Y. M. Xie, Evolutionary Topology Optimization of Continuum Structures: Methods and Applications (John Wiley & Sons, Chichester, 2010). [5]
references.relevance: BESO technique with sensitivity averaging for convergence

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Design variables need to be updated based on sensitivities. [Gap] Elements must be switched between solid and void states. [Decision] To achieve design update, chose to use Python code with ranking method to perform design variable update on all elements, expecting to obtain updated solid/void element distribution.

parameters.update_method: Ranking method (more efficient than bisection method)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Elements sorted by sensitivities in descending order. Elements corresponding to first im components set as solid, others set as void. Design variables updated according to target volume.

references.citation: Z. H. Zuo, and Y. M. Xie, A simple and compact Python code for complex 3D topology optimization, Adv. Eng. Software 85, 1 (2015). [40]
references.relevance: Previous 100-line Python code with bisection method, ranking method is more efficient

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Optimization process needs termination criteria. [Gap] Convergence must be checked to determine if optimization should continue. [Decision] To achieve proper termination, chose to use Python code to perform convergence check on structural compliance change, expecting to obtain convergence status.

parameters.convergence_criterion: Change in mean compliance over last 10 iterations

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Relative change in structural compliance calculated. Optimization terminated if change over last 10 iterations is acceptably small (τb ≤ 0.001).

references.citation: X. Huang, and Y. M. Xie, Evolutionary Topology Optimization of Continuum Structures: Methods and Applications (John Wiley & Sons, Chichester, 2010). [5]
references.relevance: BESO convergence criterion based on compliance change

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Self-weight problems require special handling of void elements. [Gap] Void elements should not contribute to self-weight in FEA. [Decision] To achieve efficient self-weight optimization, chose to use Python code with hard-kill algorithm to perform void element elimination on FEA model, expecting to obtain reduced computational complexity.

parameters.void_element_treatment: Eliminated from FEA in next iteration
parameters.readmission_condition: Through filtering if sensitivities sufficiently large
parameters.connected_region_search: Iterative process to find main connected region

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Void elements eliminated from FEA model, their self-weight not applied to optimized structure. Isolated solid elements eliminated to avoid FEA termination. Support structure optimized with tent-like shell form generated.

references.citation: X. Huang, and Y. M. Xie, Evolutionary Topology Optimization of Continuum Structures: Methods and Applications (John Wiley & Sons, Chichester, 2010). [5]

03_success_verification.validation_technique: Four numerical examples (cantilever beam, rim, canopy, support structure) with comparison to previous 100-line Python code

filter_matrix_calculation_time_reduction.interpretation: Filter matrix calculation time reduced from 45276 s (100-line code) to 11 s (proposed code)

single_iteration_time.interpretation: One iteration completed in 23 s with proposed code vs 103 s with 100-line code

total_optimization_time.interpretation: Whole optimization process takes 2097 s vs 54551 s with previous code

compliance_difference.interpretation: Difference between compliances of two designs is less than 0.1%

workstation_configuration.interpretation: Workstation used for topology optimization demonstrating scalability

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The research objective was achieved. The subdomain-based parallel processing strategy significantly accelerated the optimization process (99.9997% reduction in filter matrix calculation time, 23 s vs 103 s per iteration, 2097 s vs 54551 s total) while maintaining solution quality (<0.1% compliance difference). The Python code successfully handles three-dimensional geometries with arbitrary shapes through Abaqus CAE integration.
